content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Jessica Jahr, Staff Counsel 
State Water Resources Control Board 
Office of the Chief Counsel 
100 I "I" Street, 22nd Floor 
P. 0. Box 100 
Sacramento, CA 95812-0100 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-14-031 
Dear Ms. Jahr: 
March 3, 2014 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Dr. Jean-Pierre Wolff, a 
Board Member on the Regional Water Quality Control Board, Central Coast Region, regarding 
his duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 In 
this request, you are requesting further clarification of the Jahr Advice Letter A-l 3-045a. 
Please note that this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices 
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In 
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Moreover, our advice is based solely on the provisions of 
the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other ethics laws or rules. 
FACTS 
Dr. Wolff is a Board Member of the Regional Water Quality Control Board, Central 
Coast Region (the "Board"). In the 2013 Jahr letter we advised that that Dr. Wolff could not 
participate in decisions affecting the 2012 Agricultural Order (the "Order") of the Board based 
on Dr. Wolff's financial interest in a vineyard he owns with his wife and children that is a 
discharger under the Order. 
Your new request seeks analysis of other decisions that the Board may be required to 
make and whether Dr. Wolff has a conflict of interest in these other decisions. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 I 000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references arc to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-14-031 
Page No. 2 
1. At almost every Board meeting, there is an update on the implementation of the Order. 
The update generally involves staff members presenting one or more elements of the 
Order and explaining how staff and the dischargers are working to achieve that element. 
There typically is public comment regarding that element of the Order, potentially 
including requests for modifications to that element or other parts. In some cases, the 
Board provides no further input; in others, the Board may provide general direction to 
staff to look into an issue and make further recommendations to the board. The 
subsequent recommendations may take the form of additional staff -level actions or 
proposed board -level decisions. 
2. The Order requires regulated dischargers to take certain actions subject to Executive 
Officer (EO) approval and allows the EO to make changes to the monitoring program. 
The Board members may give more specific direction to either the EO or staff regarding 
how to implement the Order. For example, the Order allows the EO to approve group­
monitoring programs, known as cooperative monitoring, in lieu of individual monitoring. 
On March 15, 2013, the Board held an informational item on the cooperative 
groundwater monitoring programs where cooperative program proponents presented their 
proposals to the Board. During the presentation, the Board members asked questions of 
the presenters and staff but did not provide any direction to the EO or staff. The EO later 
approved two proposals and disapproved two proposals. On May 31, 2013, the Board 
held another informational item on the cooperative groundwater monitoring programs, 
and staff and the public discussed all four of the cooperative monitoring proposals. Staff 
was providing a status update and did not seek board direction. 
When the Board enacted the Order, it included Monitoring and Reporting Provisions 
(MRP) for each tier. The EO may alter the MRPs without needing the Board to vote on 
the alterations. At times, in the informational items, the Board members provide 
feedback that may result in the EO making specific modifications to the MRPs. For 
example, the Board has previously requested the EO to consider modifying the deadlines 
for permit requirements. This may apply only to the Tier 2 and Tier 3 
3. The Board may hold hearings to reconsider the EO's approval or disapproval of a 
cooperative groundwater-monitoring program or change the tier designation for a 
particular discharger or group of dischargers. These hearings would not result in any 
amendments to the Order, but may affect dischargers' responsibilities under the Order. 
The Order states that dischargers may form cooperative groundwater monitoring groups 
to fulfill the requirements to test drinking water wells. The State Water Resources 
Control Board recently adopted an order that allows future cooperative groundwater 
monitoring programs, water quality projects and tier changes to be approved or 
disapproved by the EO, with discretionary review by the Board of the EO's decision. It 
is likely that in the near future the Board will decide whether to approve or disapprove a 
cooperative groundwater-monitoring proposal. You stated that such decisions are 
separate and segmented from the Order and that any decision on the proposals or tier 


File No. A-14-031 
Page No. 3 
changes will not reopen the 2012 Order or change its potential financial effects on 
Dr. Wolff or Wolff Vineyards. You stated that Dr. Wolff is not a participant in the 
cooperative monitoring program, and there are no participants in the cooperative 
monitoring program with property within 500 feet of Wolff Vineyards. 
4. The Board could amend the Order in a manner that only affects Tier 2 and /or Tier 3 
growers. The Order is segmented into the tiers, and each tier is addressed separately in 
the Order. Amending requirements for the Tier 2 and Tier 3 growers necessitates 
reopening the Order in its entirety. You stated, however, that decisions involving Tier 2 
and Tier 3 requirements are not inextricably interrelated to Tier 1 requirements; they are 
completely different requirements that Tier 1 growers do not need to comply with and 
they will not affirm, nullify, or alter the Tier 1 requirements. Therefore, decisions 
affecting the 2012 Agricultural Order could be segmented into the three tiers. In such 
cases, there would be no financial impact to Dr. Wolff from the changes to the Tier 2 and 
Tier 3 requirements. 
QUESTIONS, CONCLUSIONS, AND ANALYSIS 
You have asked, whether Dr. Wolff, despite his conflict of interest may be involved in 
the following. 
1. Informational items presemed to the board at a public meeting involving 
implementation of the Order that do not i11voil•e specific direction from the board members to the 
EO or staff. An example of this would be the EO providing infonnation about approvals he has 
provided under authority delegated by the Order. 
As we discussed in our prior letter, a public official with a conflict of interest in a specific 
governmental decision is prohibited from making, participating in making, or influencing the 
governmental decision. Certain public officials who have a conflict of interest in a decision, 
those who hold an office specified in Section 87200, must also: ( 1) orally identify each type of 
economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest as discussed 
in Regulation 18702.S(b), on the record of the meeting: (2) recuse himself or herself; and 
(3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. 
Members of Regional Water Quality Control Boards are not listed in Section 87200, but 
such agencies may be subject to Section 87200 if they manage public investments.2 For 
? Regulation 1870l(b)(l) provides:"' Other public officials who manage public investments' means: 
(A) Members of boards and commissions, including pension and retirement boards or commissions, or of 
committees thereof, who exercise responsibility for the management of public investments; (B) High-level officers 
and employees of public agencies who exercise primary responsibility for the management of public investments, 
such as chief or principal investment officers or chief financial managers. This category shall not include officers 
and employees who work under the supervision of the chief or principal investment officers or the chief financial 
managers; and (C) Individuals who, pursuant to a contract with a state or local government agency, perform the 
same or substantially all the same functions that would otherwise be performed by the public officials described in 
subdivision (b)(l)(B) above." Regulation 1870l(b)(2) provides that "public investments" means "the investment of 


File No. A-14-031 
Page No. 4 
purposes of this letter, we assume that the members of Regional Water Quality Control Boards 
are not Section 87200 filers. 
In all other cases, a public official need merely abstain from making, participating in 
making, or influencing the decision in which the official has a conflict-of-interest. A public 
official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the authority of his or 
her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of 
action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 
18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the 
authority of his or her position and without significant substantive review, the official negotiates, 
advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the governmental decision. 
(Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to 
influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any 
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) 
Thus, an official with a conflict of interest does not violate Section 87100 by merely 
hearing an informational item presented to the entire board at a public meeting. 
2. Jnformatio11a/ items i1111olvi11g implementation of the Order, where the board directs 
the EO or staff to take specific actions. An example of this would be the board directing the EO 
as part of an infon11ational item to bring a future item to the board for board member 
consideration /action. 
Similarly, decisions that will have no financial impact on the Board Member or his or her 
interests are not the basis for a conflict of interest. For example, Regulation I 8705(c) provides: 
"[A]n official does not have to disqualify himself or herself from a governmental decision if ... 
the decision will have no financial effect on the person or business entity who appears before the 
official, or on the real property in which the official holds a direct or indirect interest, or on the 
personal finances of the official and/or his immediate family." Moreover, Regulation 18702.4(a) 
provides a specific exception from the conflict of interest rules for actions of public officials that 
are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual, or clerical. Obviously, the determination of whether 
the exceptions apply will need to be made on a decision-by-decision basis. 
3. A hearing to review the EO's approval or denial of a proposal, project, or tier change 
that affects a discharger's responsibilities under the Order bur does not amend the Order. A11 
example of this is the Board holding a hearing to consider approval of the Ce1ltral Coast 
Groundwater Monitoring Coalition's group monitoring program and to detennine whether 
public moneys in real estate, securities, or other economic interests for the production of revenue or other financial 
return" and Regulation 18701(b)(3) provides that "public moneys" means "all moneys belonging to, received by, or 
held by, the state, or any city, county, town, district, or public agency therein, or by an officer thereof acting in his or 
her official capacity, and includes the proceeds of all bonds and other evidences of indebtedness, trust funds held by 
public pension and retirement systems, deferred compensation funds held for investment by public agencies, and 
public moneys held by a financial institution under a trust indenture to which a public agency is a party." 


File No. A-14-031 
Page No. 5 
participating dischargers' can use the Coalition's monitoring data to fulfill the dischargers' 
monitoring requirements in the Order. 
The types of decisions discussed in your question appear to be specific applications by 
dischargers for approval of the discharger's plan or project You stated that, in compliance with 
the Order, regulated dischargers and other stakeholders may be required to obtain certain 
approvals. You stated that these decisions would not result in any revision to the Order or tier 
requirements as applied to Dr. Wolff that were established in the adoption of the Order in 2012. 
Rather, these decisions merely "implement" the Order concerning a specific user. 
As you note in your letter, the Commission has long advised that "a public official 
disqualified from one decision may participate in other potentially related decisions provided that 
the official's participation does not affect the decision in which he or she has a conflict of 
interest." (/11 re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) In these cases, related decisions may be 
segmented from the decision in which the official has a conflict of interest so long as the action 
taken in the related decision will not effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of 
the decision in which the official has a conflict of interest. 
Moreover, decisions that merely "implement" decisions that have already been made do 
not create a conflict of interest so long as the remaining decisions will not result in reopening or 
in any way affect the decisions from which the official was disqualified and the remaining 
decisions will not independently have a material financial effect on the official's interests. 
(Wame Advice Letter, No. I-02-052; Olson Advice Letter, No. A-00-237.) 
Regulation 18709 provides the rules for "segmenting" a governmental decision: 
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a 
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the 
following conditions apply: 
"( 1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be 
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the 
decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented 
from the other decisions; 
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is 
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the 
disqualified official's participation in any way; and 
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has 
been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a 


File No. A-14-031 
Page No. 6 
reopening of or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official 
was disqualified. 
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably 
interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, 
nullify, or alter the result of another decision." 
In this case, the decisions you describe are implementation and compliance decisions that will 
not result in reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which the official was disqualified 
(the adoption or amendment of the 2012 Order). While these decisions will still need to be 
analyzed to determine whether there will be a conflict of interest based on the facts of the 
specific decision,3 assuming that it does not, Dr. Wolff could participate in the decision. 
4. Amendmellts to the 2012 Agricultural Order that only affect Tier 2 and /or Tier 3 
growers, where none of the affected growers are within 500 feet of Dr. Wolff's property. An 
example of this would be a hearing to consider amending the nitrogen reporting requiremellts in 
the 2012 Agricultural Order for Tier 2 and Tier 3 growers. 
This is a more difficult question. Theoretically, were Tier 1 amendment decisions to be 
made and finalized, Dr. Wolff would be able to participate in the decisions on the other two 
Tiers. The fact question that would need to be answered would be whether the decisions on the 
three Tiers might be "inextricably interrelated" in that the result of one decision will effectively 
determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision. For example, we have said 
under some circumstances a decision regarding a grant (grant A) for which the source of income 
has 1101 applied may be "inextricably interrelated" to a decision regarding another grant (grant B) 
for which it did apply because the decision regarding grant A will increase or decrease the pool 
of funds available for grants and reduce the chance of the source of income will get grant B. 
(See e.g., Hopper Advice Letter, No. 1-1 1-100.) Applied to your facts, if the three tiers were 
interrelated such that a decision to establish more controls on one tier would permit the loosening 
of controls on another tier (for example), those decisions would be inextricably interrelated. 
You stated that the Order is segmented into the tiers, and each tier is addressed separately 
in the Order and that decisions involving Tier 2 and Tier 3 requirements are completely different 
requirements that Tier 1 growers do not need to comply with and they will not affirm, nullify, or 
alter the Tier 1 requirements. Under these facts, amendment decisions regarding the Order could 
be segmented and Dr. Wolff would be allowed to participate in Tier 2 and Tier 3 amendment 
decisions. 
3 For example, a Tier 2 or 3 discharger applying for an approval might be located near the Dr. Wolffs 
property that would create a conflict of interest on its own facts based on the proximity of the applicant's property to 
the Wolff Vineyard. You stated in your letter that Dr. Wolff would not participate in modifications that would affect 
him or real property within 500 feet of Wolff Vineyards. 


File No. A-14-031 
Page No. 7 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
eneral Counsel

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: S, CONCLUSIONS, AND ANALYSIS 
You have asked, whether Dr. Wolff, despite his conflict of interest may be involved in 
the following. 
1. Informational items presemed to the board at a public meeting involving 
implementation of the Order that do not i11voil•e specific direction from the board members to the 
EO or staff. An example of this would be the EO providing infonnation about approvals he has 
provided under authority delegated by the Order. 
As we discussed in our prior letter, a public official with a conflict of interest in a specific 
governmental decision is prohibited from making, participating in making, or influencing the 
governmental decision. Certain public officials who have a conflict of interest in a decision, 
those who hold an office specified in Section 87200, must also: ( 1) orally identify each type of 
economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest as discussed 
in Regulation 18702.S(b), on the record of the meeting: (2) recuse himself or herself; and 
(3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. 
Members of Regional Water Quality Control Boards are not listed in Section 87200, but 
such agencies may be subject to Section 87200 if they manage public investments.2 For 
? Regulation 1870l(b)(l) provides:"' Other public officials who manage public investments' means: 
(A) Members of boards and commissions, including pension and retirement boards or commissions, or of 
committees thereof, who exercise responsibility for the management of public investments; (B) High-level officers 
and employees of public agencies who exercise primary responsibility for the management of public investments, 
such as chief or principal investment officers or chief financial managers. This category shall not include officers 
and employees who work under the supervision of the chief or principal investment officers or the chief financial 
managers; and (C) Individuals who, pursuant to a contract with a state or local government agency, perform the 
same or substantially all the same functions that would otherwise be performed by the public officials described in 
subdivision (b)(l)(B) above." Regulation 1870l(b)(2) provides that "public investments" means "the investment of  
purposes of this letter, we assume that the members of Regional Water Quality Control Boards 
are not Section 87200 filers. 
In all other cases, a public official need merely abstain from making, participating in 
making, or influencing the decision in which the official has a conflict-of-interest. A public 
official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the authority of his or 
her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of 
action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 
18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the 
authority of his or her position and without significant substantive review, the official negotiates, 
advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the governmental decision. 
(Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to 
influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any 
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) 
Thus, an official with a conflict of interest does not violate Section 87100 by merely 
hearing an informational item presented to the entire board at a public meeting. 
2. Jnformatio11a/ items i1111olvi11g implementation of the Order, where the board directs 
the EO or staff to take specific actions. An example of this would be the board directing the EO 
as part of an infon11ational item to bring a future item to the board for board member 
consideration /action. 
Similarly, decisions that will have no financial impact on the Board Member or his or her 
interests are not the basis for a conflict of interest. For example, Regulation I 8705(c) provides: 
"[A]n official does not have to disqualify himself or herself from a governmental decision if ... 
the decision will have no financial effect on the person or business entity who appears before the 
official, or on the real property in which the official holds a direct or indirect interest, or on the 
personal finances of the official and/or his immediate family." Moreover, Regulation 18702.4(a) 
provides a specific exception from the conflict of interest rules for actions of public officials that 
are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual, or clerical. Obviously, the determination of whether 
the exceptions apply will need to be made on a decision-by-decision basis. 
3. A hearing to review the EO's approval or denial of a proposal, project, or tier change 
that affects a discharger's responsibilities under the Order bur does not amend the Order. A11 
example of this is the Board holding a hearing to consider approval of the Ce1ltral Coast 
Groundwater Monitoring Coalition's group monitoring program and to detennine whether 
public moneys in real estate, securities, or other economic interests for the production of revenue or other financial 
return" and Regulation 18701(b)(3) provides that "public moneys" means "all moneys belonging to, received by, or 
held by, the state, or any city, county, town, district, or public agency therein, or by an officer thereof acting in his or 
her official capacity, and includes the proceeds of all bonds and other evidences of indebtedness, trust funds held by 
public pension and retirement systems, deferred compensation funds held for investment by public agencies, and 
public moneys held by a financial institution under a trust indenture to which a public agency is a party."  
participating dischargers' can use the Coalition's monitoring data to fulfill the dischargers' 
monitoring requirements in the Order. 
The types of decisions discussed in your question appear to be specific applications by 
dischargers for approval of the discharger's plan or project You stated that, in compliance with 
the Order, regulated dischargers and other stakeholders may be required to obtain certain 
approvals. You stated that these decisions would not result in any revision to the Order or tier 
requirements as applied to Dr. Wolff that were established in the adoption of the Order in 2012. 
Rather, these decisions merely "implement" the Order concerning a specific user. 
As you note in your letter, the Commission has long advised that "a public official 
disqualified from one decision may participate in other potentially related decisions provided that 
the official's participation does not affect the decision in which he or she has a conflict of 
interest." (/11 re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) In these cases, related decisions may be 
segmented from the decision in which the official has a conflict of interest so long as the action 
taken in the related decision will not effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of 
the decision in which the official has a conflict of interest. 
Moreover, decisions that merely "implement" decisions that have already been made do 
not create a conflict of interest so long as the remaining decisions will not result in reopening or 
in any way affect the decisions from which the official was disqualified and the remaining 
decisions will not independently have a material financial effect on the official's interests. 
(Wame Advice Letter, No. I-02-052; Olson Advice Letter, No. A-00-237.) 
Regulation 18709 provides the rules for "segmenting" a governmental decision: 
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a 
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the 
following conditions apply: 
"( 1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be 
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the 
decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented 
from the other decisions; 
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is 
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the 
disqualified official's participation in any way; and 
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has 
been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a  
reopening of or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official 
was disqualified. 
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably 
interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, 
nullify, or alter the result of another decision." 
In this case, the decisions you describe are implementation and compliance decisions that will 
not result in reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which the official was disqualified 
(the adoption or amendment of the 2012 Order). While these decisions will still need to be 
analyzed to determine whether there will be a conflict of interest based on the facts of the 
specific decision,3 assuming that it does not, Dr. Wolff could participate in the decision. 
4. Amendmellts to the 2012 Agricultural Order that only affect Tier 2 and /or Tier 3 
growers, where none of the affected growers are within 500 feet of Dr. Wolff's property. An 
example of this would be a hearing to consider amending the nitrogen reporting requiremellts in 
the 2012 Agricultural Order for Tier 2 and Tier 3 growers. 
This is a more difficult question. Theoretically, were Tier 1 amendment decisions to be 
made and finalized, Dr. Wolff would be able to participate in the decisions on the other two 
Tiers. The fact question that would need to be answered would be whether the decisions on the 
three Tiers might be "inextricably interrelated" in that the result of one decision will effectively 
determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision. For example, we have said 
under some circumstances a decision regarding a grant (grant A) for which the source of income 
has 1101 applied may be "inextricably interrelated" to a decision regarding another grant (grant B) 
for which it did apply because the decision regarding grant A will increase or decrease the pool 
of funds available for grants and reduce the chance of the source of income will get grant B. 
(See e.g., Hopper Advice Letter, No. 1-1 1-100.) Applied to your facts, if the three tiers were 
interrelated such that a decision to establish more controls on one tier would permit the loosening 
of controls on another tier (for example), those decisions would be inextricably interrelated. 
You stated that the Order is segmented into the tiers, and each tier is addressed separately 
in the Order and that decisions involving Tier 2 and Tier 3 requirements are completely different 
requirements that Tier 1 growers do not need to comply with and they will not affirm, nullify, or 
alter the Tier 1 requirements. Under these facts, amendment decisions regarding the Order could 
be segmented and Dr. Wolff would be allowed to participate in Tier 2 and Tier 3 amendment 
decisions. 
3 For example, a Tier 2 or 3 discharger applying for an approval might be located near the Dr. Wolffs 
property that would create a conflict of interest on its own facts based on the proximity of the applicant's property to 
the Wolff Vineyard. You stated in your letter that Dr. Wolff would not participate in modifications that would affect 
him or real property within 500 feet of Wolff Vineyards.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Jessica Jahr, Staff Counsel State Water Resources Control Board Office of the Chief Counsel 100 I "I" Street, 22nd Floor P. 0. Box 100 Sacramento, CA 95812-0100 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-14-031 Dear Ms. Jahr: March 3, 2014 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Dr. Jean-Pierre Wolff, a Board Member on the Regional Water Quality Control Board, Central Coast Region, regarding his duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 In this request, you are requesting further clarification of the Jahr Advice Letter A-l 3-045a. Please note that this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Moreover, our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other ethics laws or rules. FACTS Dr. Wolff is a Board Member of the Regional Water Quality Control Board, Central Coast Region (the "Board"). In the 2013 Jahr letter we advised that that Dr. Wolff could not participate in decisions affecting the 2012 Agricultural Order (the "Order") of the Board based on Dr. Wolff's financial interest in a vineyard he owns with his wife and children that is a discharger under the Order. Your new request seeks analysis of other decisions that the Board may be required to make and whether Dr. Wolff has a conflict of interest in these other decisions. 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 I 000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references arc to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-14-031 Page No. 2 1. At almost every Board meeting, there is an update on the implementation of the Order. The update generally involves staff members presenting one or more elements of the Order and explaining how staff and the dischargers are working to achieve that element. There typically is public comment regarding that element of the Order, potentially including requests for modifications to that element or other parts. In some cases, the Board provides no further input; in others, the Board may provide general direction to staff to look into an issue and make further recommendations to the board. The subsequent recommendations may take the form of additional staff -level actions or proposed board -level decisions. 2. The Order requires regulated dischargers to take certain actions subject to Executive Officer (EO) approval and allows the EO to make changes to the monitoring program. The Board members may
